title: Trees & BST
summary: Trees are your strength — this module converts that comfort into OA speed. One idea carries almost everything: most tree problems are a single DFS where every node returns a small packet of info to its parent. Learn to see that packet and you solve 80% of tree problems on sight.

notes.heading: Spotting the tree pattern in 20 seconds
notes.body:
You're already comfortable with trees, so this module isn't about learning them — it's about *recognition speed*. The goal: read a statement and know, within 20 seconds, which of exactly **two engines** to start typing.

Reach for this module when:

- The input is a **binary tree / BST** (`TreeNode*`), or something implicitly tree-shaped: parent pointers, nested or hierarchical structure.
- The question is about **paths, depth, diameter, subtree properties, ancestors,** or **level-by-level** aggregation.

The statement's *phrasing* usually names the engine for you:

| Phrase in the statement | Engine to reach for |
|---|---|
| "deepest / longest / kth / lowest common…", "is this valid / balanced / symmetric…", "for each node compute…" | one recursive **DFS** |
| "level order", "row by row", "zigzag", "right side view" | **BFS** with a queue |
| "BST" anywhere in the statement | exploit sorted inorder or the `left < root < right` invariant |

> [!EXAM] Microsoft loves trees
> Trees are a STAR topic on Codility-style screens. A tree medium is often the *fastest* medium on the paper — if you recognize the pattern instantly, you bank ten minutes to spend on the harder question.

> [!KEY]
> Most tree problems are **one DFS where every node returns a small packet of info to its parent**. The whole skill is learning to name that packet before you write a line of code.

notes.heading: Engine 1 — DFS and the three orders
THE pattern — return a packet up, report to a global
notes.body:
Here is the single most reused tree idea in interviews. Each recursive call returns a **compact packet** summarizing its subtree; the parent combines the two children's packets. Sometimes the final answer isn't the thing you return — then you also keep a **global** that you update as you pass through each node.

The canonical example is diameter: return *height*, report *bent paths*:

```cpp
// ---------- THE pattern: return info up the recursion ----------
// Example packet: height of subtree, while updating a global answer (diameter).
int best = 0;
int height(TreeNode* node) {
    if (!node) return 0;                 // base case: empty subtree
    int L = height(node->left);          // ask left child for its info
    int R = height(node->right);         // ask right child for its info
    best = max(best, L + R);             // combine here: path through THIS node
    return 1 + max(L, R);                // return MY info to my parent
}
```

[[fig:m06-report-return]]

> [!ANALOGY]
> Think of a company org chart. Each manager asks their two reports: "how deep is your team?" A manager can *tell their own boss* only one straight chain (their taller side plus themselves). But the interesting fact — "the longest chain of communication *bends* at me, going down one side and up the other" — can't be passed upward as-is. So the manager files it in a shared record (`best`) instead.

Run the **mental checklist** before writing any tree DFS:

1. What is the **base case** (usually `nullptr` → return the identity value)?
2. What **packet** does each subtree return to its parent?
3. How do I **combine** the two children's packets?
4. Is the answer I **return** the same as the answer I **report** — or do I need a global?

> [!KEY] Return vs. report
> Question 4 is the separator. *Height*: return == report, no global needed. *Diameter / max path sum*: the best answer **bends** at some node, and a bent path can't be extended upward — so you report it to a global and return only the straight, extendable arm `1 + max(L, R)`.

> [!EXAMPLE]
> Tree `4 — 2 — 5` under root `1` (node 2 has children 4 and 5). At node 2: `L = 1`, `R = 1`. Report `best = max(best, 1+1) = 2` (path 4→2→5 bends here). Return `1 + max(1,1) = 2` to node 1 — the straight arm 4→2 (or 5→2) that node 1 *can* extend.

notes.heading: Engine 2 — BFS, one level at a time
BSTs — the word itself is the hint
notes.body:
When a statement says **BST**, that word is planted deliberately. The interviewer wants to see whether you exploit the structure ($O(h)$ or a sorted stream) instead of writing generic tree code ($O(n)$ with no order used). Two facts cover almost every BST problem:

1. **Inorder traversal of a BST is sorted, strictly increasing.** "Kth smallest" = inorder, stop at the kth. "Validate" = inorder must never step down. "Two-sum in a BST" = inorder to a sorted array, then two pointers.
2. **Every node lives inside an interval `(low, high)` inherited from its ancestors.** Going left tightens the *upper* bound to the node's value; going right tightens the *lower* bound. This is how you validate correctly, and how you binary-search your way down in $O(h)$ (e.g. LCA in a BST: walk down until `p` and `q` split).

[[fig:m06-bst-bounds]]

> [!PITFALL] The local-check trap
> Checking only `left->val < val < right->val` at each node is the most common wrong answer to Validate BST. It passes trees that are *not* BSTs: root 5 with right child 6, where 6 has left child 3. Every parent-child triple looks fine locally, but 3 sits in 5's **right** subtree, so globally it must be > 5. Only inherited bounds catch this.

> [!WARNING] Integer bounds
> Node values can be `INT_MIN` / `INT_MAX`, so seed the bounds with `long` (`LONG_MIN`, `LONG_MAX`) — with `int` bounds the comparison false-fails at the extremes. The inorder alternative sidesteps this with a `TreeNode*` "previous" pointer instead of a sentinel value.

notes.heading: Pitfalls, and the 20-second narration
notes.body:
The recurring bugs in tree code are few and well known — check them off consciously:

- **Null checks first.** `if (!node) return <base>;` before touching `node->anything`. Forgetting it is the #1 crash. Also handle `root == nullptr` at the top level.
- **Edges vs. nodes.** For diameter / path-length problems, decide up front whether the answer counts *nodes* or *edges* and stay consistent (LeetCode's diameter counts **edges**). This off-by-one is the classic silent wrong-answer.
- **Report-vs-return.** If the best answer can bend at a node, you need a global. Saying this out loud in an interview signals you understand the *pattern*, not just the problem.
- **BFS level freeze.** `int sz = q.size();` before the inner loop, every time.
- **Construct-from-traversal:** pass indices/ranges, never sliced vectors — slicing costs $O(n)$ per call and turns the build into $O(n^2)$. A hashmap from value → inorder index gives $O(1)$ splits.

> [!WARNING] Recursion depth
> A skewed tree gives $O(n)$ stack depth. That's fine at OA sizes, but be ready to *mention* that you could convert to an explicit stack if someone asks about a million-node degenerate tree — the mention is usually all they want.

> [!EXAM] The 20-second narration
> Best framing under time pressure: *"I'll do one DFS. Each node returns **[X]** to its parent; I combine the children with **[Y]**; and I track **[global answer]** as I go."* Fill in X, Y and the global, and you've essentially written the solution before touching the keyboard — exactly the speed Microsoft and Flipkart OAs reward.

problems.name: Maximum Depth of Binary Tree
problems.note: The "hello world" of return-info-up — height in 3 lines.

problems.name: Invert Binary Tree
problems.note: Post-order structural mutation: swap children on the way up.

problems.name: Same Tree
problems.note: Parallel recursion over two trees at once.

problems.name: Symmetric Tree
problems.note: Mirror recursion: compare (L.left, R.right) and (L.right, R.left).

problems.name: Balanced Binary Tree
problems.note: Return height AND balance flag in one pass — the -1 sentinel trick.

problems.name: Binary Tree Level Order Traversal
problems.note: Canonical BFS with the `sz = q.size()` level freeze.

problems.name: Binary Tree Right Side View
problems.note: BFS, take the last node of each level (or DFS right-first by depth).

problems.name: Diameter of Binary Tree
problems.note: Global max while returning height — the report-vs-return distinction.

problems.name: Validate Binary Search Tree
problems.note: Pass down (min, max) bounds — the classic local-check trap.

problems.name: Lowest Common Ancestor of a Binary Search Tree
problems.note: Exploit BST ordering to walk down without recursion — O(h).

problems.name: Lowest Common Ancestor of a Binary Tree
problems.note: General LCA via the post-order "found-in-subtree" signal.

problems.name: Kth Smallest Element in a BST
problems.note: Inorder gives sorted order — stop at the kth.

problems.name: Construct Binary Tree from Preorder and Inorder Traversal
problems.note: Preorder gives the root; inorder splits left/right — use index ranges, not sliced vectors.

problems.name: Binary Tree Maximum Path Sum
problems.note: The boss: global max bent path vs. the straight arm you may return to a parent.

problems.name: Serialize and Deserialize Binary Tree
problems.note: Preorder with null markers; rebuild via a consumed cursor.

deepdives.q: **Diameter of Binary Tree** — return the length (in edges) of the longest path between any two nodes in the tree. The path may or may not pass through the root.
**Binary Tree Maximum Path Sum** — node values may be negative; find the maximum sum over all paths, where a path can start and end at any nodes and never revisits a node.
deepdives.answer:
**Why naive fails.** Summing `L + node + R` and returning it fails for the same reason as diameter — you can't hand a bent path to your parent. And there's a new subtlety: values can be **negative**, so blindly including a child's subtree can make your path *worse*.

> [!INTUITION] Drop losing arms
> An arm with negative total only drags the sum down. Attaching *nothing* (contribution 0) beats attaching a negative arm — so clamp each child's gain at zero.

**The trick, step by step:**

1. From each child, take the best *downward* gain, clamped: `int L = max(0, gain(n->left));`.
2. **Report** the best bent path through this node: `L + n->val + R` — a complete candidate path.
3. **Return** the best straight arm your parent can extend: `n->val + max(L, R)`.

```cpp
int best = INT_MIN;
int gain(TreeNode* n) {
    if (!n) return 0;
    int L = max(0, gain(n->left));      // drop negative arms
    int R = max(0, gain(n->right));
    best = max(best, n->val + L + R);   // REPORT full path through n
    return n->val + max(L, R);          // RETURN extendable arm
}
```

> [!PITFALL]
> Initialize `best = INT_MIN`, **not** 0. The tree may be a single negative node, and that lone node is a valid path — starting at 0 would silently "win" with a path that doesn't exist.

**Complexity:** $O(n)$ time, $O(h)$ stack.

**What generalizes:** the diameter skeleton plus two moves — clamp negative contributions, and make sure the global's initial value can't beat a legal answer. Both moves recur across hard tree problems.

deepdives.q: **Validate Binary Search Tree** — determine whether a binary tree satisfies the BST property: every node in a left subtree is smaller than its ancestor, every node in a right subtree larger.
**Lowest Common Ancestor of a Binary Tree** — given two nodes `p` and `q` in a general binary tree (no BST ordering, no parent pointers), find their lowest common ancestor.
deepdives.answer:
**Why naive fails.** With no BST ordering you can't steer by comparing values, and without parent pointers you can't walk upward. Computing full root-to-node paths for both targets works but costs extra passes and storage.

> [!INTUITION] The split point
> The LCA is the *deepest* node that has `p` in one subtree and `q` in the other — or that **is** one of them, with the other somewhere below. So make the recursion answer one question: "did this subtree find `p` or `q`?"

**How the recursion encodes it:** `lca(node)` returns non-null if `p` or `q` is found in that subtree. At each node, look at what the two children report:

- **Both** children return non-null → `p` and `q` sit on opposite sides → **this node is the LCA**.
- **One** child returns non-null → bubble that pointer upward; the LCA (if any) is higher, or is that returned node itself.
- Also, if the current node *is* `p` or `q`, return it immediately — if the other target is below, this node is the split point anyway.

[[fig:m06-lca-signal]]

```cpp
TreeNode* lca(TreeNode* n, TreeNode* p, TreeNode* q) {
    if (!n || n == p || n == q) return n;    // found one, or dead end
    TreeNode* L = lca(n->left,  p, q);
    TreeNode* R = lca(n->right, p, q);
    if (L && R) return n;                     // p and q on opposite sides -> LCA
    return L ? L : R;                         // bubble up the single hit
}
```

**Complexity:** one post-order pass, $O(n)$ time, $O(h)$ stack, no parent pointers, no extra storage.

**What generalizes:** for the **BST variant** it's even easier — walk from the root: both `p, q <` node → go left; both `>` → go right; otherwise you're at the split point. That's $O(h)$ with no recursion at all. The broader lesson: a return value can carry a *search signal*, not just a number.

quiz.q: An OA asks: *"Return the values visible when the tree is viewed from the right side, top to bottom."* Which approach fits most naturally?
options: Post-order DFS, returning the max value of each subtree
Inorder traversal, keeping every other value
BFS level order, keeping the last node of each level
Pre-order DFS with a stack of ancestors
quiz.explanation: "Viewed from the right, top to bottom" is a per-level question in disguise: the rightmost node of each level is exactly what you'd see. BFS with the size freeze hands you each level as a batch — keep the last element. (A right-first DFS recording the first node seen at each depth also works, but it's the same insight.) The distractors don't respect levels at all: subtree maxima and inorder order say nothing about visibility.

quiz.q: In the BFS template you replace `int sz = q.size(); for (int i = 0; i < sz; ++i)` with `for (int i = 0; i < q.size(); ++i)`. What actually happens?
options: Nothing — the two loops are equivalent
Children pushed during the loop grow `q.size()` mid-flight, so nodes from the next level leak into the current one
The loop exits early and skips half of each level
It infinite-loops on any non-empty tree
quiz.explanation: Every `q.push(child)` increases `q.size()`, and since the loop re-reads it each iteration, the loop keeps running into the next level's nodes — your "levels" become a smear of mixed depths. It terminates (leaves push nothing), so it's not an infinite loop; it's worse: it runs and returns wrong groupings. Freeze `sz` before the loop, always.

quiz.q: In the diameter DFS, what does a node return **to its parent**?
options: `1 + max(L, R)` — its longest straight downward arm
`L + R` — the longest path bending at this node
The current value of the global `best`
`max(L, R)` — the taller child's height unchanged
quiz.explanation: The parent can only extend a *straight* path, so you return the longest downward arm: this node plus its taller side, `1 + max(L, R)`. The bent path `L + R` is a complete candidate answer, but it can't be extended upward — it goes to the global `best` instead. Returning `max(L, R)` forgets to count the current node, an off-by-one that shrinks every arm.

quiz.q: A BST is built by inserting 8, 3, 10, 1, 6, 14, 4, 7 in that order. What is the 3rd smallest value?
quiz.explanation: You don't need to trace the shape at all — inorder traversal of any BST yields sorted order, so the 3rd smallest is just the 3rd value in sorted order: 1, 3, **4**, 6, 7, 8, 10, 14. That shortcut *is* the Kth Smallest pattern: run inorder, decrement k at each node, stop when it hits zero.

quiz.q: A candidate validates a BST by checking, at every node, `left->val < val && val < right->val` (when children exist). Which tree exposes the bug?
options: Root 2 with left child 1 and right child 3
Root 5 with right child 6, where 6 has left child 3
A single node with value INT_MAX
A perfect BST of 7 nodes
quiz.explanation: Locally everything passes: 3 < 6 looks fine at node 6. But 3 lives in 5's *right* subtree, so the global BST rule demands 3 > 5 — violated. Only bounds inherited from **all** ancestors (or a strictly-increasing inorder check) catch this. Options A and D are valid BSTs, and a single node passes any local check legitimately.

quiz.q: Complexities of finding the LCA of two nodes: the general binary-tree recursion vs. the walk-down approach on a BST?
options: $O(\log n)$ for both
$O(n)$ for both — you must see every node either way
$O(n)$ general, $O(h)$ for the BST walk-down
$O(h)$ general, $O(n)$ for the BST
quiz.explanation: The general tree gives no steering information, so the recursion may visit all $n$ nodes. A BST tells you at every node which side both targets live on (both smaller → left, both larger → right), so you walk one root-to-split-point path: $O(h)$. Note $O(h)$ is only $O(\log n)$ if the tree is balanced — a skewed BST makes it $O(n)$, which is why option A is wrong.

quiz.q: In Binary Tree Maximum Path Sum, why does the template take `int L = max(0, gain(n->left));` instead of using the child's gain directly?
options: A negative arm can only lower any path's total, so attaching nothing (0) is always at least as good
It prevents integer overflow when summing long paths
It's the null check — a missing child returns a negative sentinel
It guarantees the returned value is positive so the recursion terminates
quiz.explanation: Paths may start and end anywhere, so you're never *forced* to include a subtree. If a child's best downward gain is negative, including it strictly hurts — clamping to 0 means "attach nothing." It has nothing to do with overflow or termination, and the null case is handled separately by `if (!n) return 0;`. Careful though: the *global* still starts at `INT_MIN`, because a lone negative node is a valid path.

m06-traversal-orders.caption: One recursion, three orders — the only difference is **when** the node is recorded relative to its child calls. Note the in-order sequence: on a BST it comes out sorted.
m06-traversal-orders.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 660 300" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="A five-node tree with its pre-order, in-order and post-order visit sequences"><text x="20" y="24" class="t-bold">one tree, three visit orders</text><line x1="162" y1="94" x2="110" y2="144" class="s-line"/><line x1="182" y1="94" x2="232" y2="144" class="s-line"/><line x1="92" y1="174" x2="64" y2="224" class="s-line"/><line x1="110" y1="174" x2="138" y2="224" class="s-line"/><circle cx="172" cy="80" r="17" class="f-panel s-line"/><text x="172" y="85" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono">1</text><circle cx="100" cy="160" r="17" class="f-panel s-line"/><text x="100" y="165" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono">2</text><circle cx="242" cy="160" r="17" class="f-panel s-line"/><text x="242" y="165" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono">3</text><circle cx="56" cy="240" r="17" class="f-panel s-line"/><text x="56" y="245" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono">4</text><circle cx="146" cy="240" r="17" class="f-panel s-line"/><text x="146" y="245" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono">5</text><text x="350" y="84" class="t-bold t-sm">PRE-order (node, L, R)</text><text x="350" y="108" class="t-mono">1 2 4 5 3</text><text x="350" y="154" class="t-bold t-sm">IN-order (L, node, R)</text><text x="350" y="178" class="t-mono t-accent">4 2 5 1 3</text><text x="490" y="178" class="t-sm t-muted">BST → sorted!</text><text x="350" y="224" class="t-bold t-sm">POST-order (L, R, node)</text><text x="350" y="248" class="t-mono t-accent2">4 5 2 3 1</text><text x="490" y="248" class="t-sm t-muted">children first</text></svg>

m06-report-return.caption: The report-vs-return split at node 2: the **bent path** 4–2–5 (highlighted) is reported to the global `best`, while only the **straight arm** `1 + max(L, R)` travels up to the parent (dashed arrow).

m06-bfs-levels.caption: BFS processes the tree in horizontal bands. At the start of each `while` iteration the queue holds **exactly one level** — freeze `sz = q.size()` and pop exactly that many while the children queue up behind.

x="430" y="244" class="t-sm t-muted">pop exactly 2; children</text><text x="430" y="268" class="t-sm t-muted">4, 5, 6 wait behind them</text></svg>

m06-bst-bounds.caption: The local-check trap: at node 6, the check `3 < 6` passes — but node 3 sits in 5's **right** subtree, so its inherited interval is `(5, 6)` and the value 3 is illegal. Only bounds carried down from all ancestors catch this.
m06-bst-bounds.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 660 310" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="A non-BST that passes local parent-child checks: root 5, right child 6, whose left child 3 violates the inherited bounds"><text x="20" y="24" class="t-bold">why local checks lie</text><line x1="188" y1="102" x2="110" y2="156" class="s-line"/><line x1="212" y1="102" x2="290" y2="156" class="s-line"/><line x1="288" y1="186" x2="250" y2="238" class="s-line"/><circle cx="200" cy="88" r="17" class="f-panel s-line"/><text x="200" y="93" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono">5</text><text x="200" y="56" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">(−∞, +∞)</text><circle cx="98" cy="170" r="17" class="f-panel s-line"/><text x="98" y="175" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono">2</text><text x="98" y="210" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">(−∞, 5)</text><circle cx="300" cy="170" r="17" class="f-panel s-line"/><text x="300" y="175" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono">6</text><text x="354" y="175" class="t-sm t-muted">(5, +∞)</text><circle cx="242" cy="252" r="17" class="f-bad-soft s-bad"/><text x="242" y="257" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono t-bad">3</text><text x="242" y="292" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-bad">must be in (5, 6) — 3 fails</text><text x="460" y="84" class="t-bold t-sm">local check at 6:</text><text x="460" y="110" class="t-ok">3 < 6 ✓ looks fine</text><text x="460" y="150" class="t-bold t-sm">global rule:</text><text x="460" y="176" class="t-sm">3 is in 5’s right subtree,</text><text x="460" y="202" class="t-bad">so 3 must be > 5 ✗</text></svg>

m06-lca-signal.caption: The post-order "found signal": nodes 6 (`p`) and 2 (`q`) each bubble a non-null pointer up (dashed arrows). Node 5 is the first node hearing hits from **both** sides — the split point, hence the LCA.
m06-lca-signal.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 660 310" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="LCA via post-order signals: p and q bubble non-null returns up, and the first node with hits on both sides is the LCA"><text x="20" y="24" class="t-bold">LCA = first node hit from both sides</text><line x1="228" y1="94" x2="152" y2="146" class="s-line"/><line x1="252" y1="94" x2="328" y2="146" class="s-line"/><line x1="128" y1="174" x2="92" y2="226" class="s-line"/><line x1="152" y1="174" x2="188" y2="226" class="s-line"/><circle cx="240" cy="80" r="17" class="f-panel s-line"/><text x="240" y="85" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono">3</text><circle cx="140" cy="160" r="17" class="f-ok-soft s-ok"/><text x="140" y="165" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono t-ok">5</text><text x="140" y="128" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-ok">LCA</text><circle cx="340" cy="160" r="17" class="f-panel s-line"/><text x="340" y="165" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono">1</text><circle cx="80" cy="240" r="17" class="f-warn-soft s-line"/><text x="80" y="245" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono">6</text><text x="80" y="280" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">p</text><circle cx="200" cy="240" r="17" class="f-warn-soft s-line"/><text x="200" y="245" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono">2</text><text x="200" y="280" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">q</text><line x1="96" y1="222" x2="120" y2="188" class="s-accent2" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="5 4" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-accent2)"/><line x1="212" y1="222" x2="172" y2="186" class="s-accent2" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="5 4" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-accent2)"/><line x1="172" y1="142" x2="216" y2="106" class="s-ok" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="5 4" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-ok)"/><text x="430" y="84" class="t-bold t-sm">at node 5:</text><text x="430" y="110" class="t-sm">left returns 6 (a hit)</text><text x="430" y="136" class="t-sm">right returns 2 (a hit)</text><text x="430" y="170" class="t-ok">both non-null → 5 is the LCA</text><text x="430" y="210" class="t-bold t-sm">at node 3:</text><text x="430" y="236" class="t-sm t-muted">only left is non-null →</text><text x="430" y="262" class="t-sm t-muted">bubble the pointer 5 upward</text></svg>
